perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that due to the sensitive nature of the Confidential Informati on, any breach of this Agreement would cause irreparable ha rm to the Disclosing Party and that the Disclosing Party shall therefore be entitled to equitable relief in additi on to all other remedies available at law.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that due to the sensitive nature of the Confidential Information, any breach of this Agreement would cause harm to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the phrase 'irreparable' and 'that the Disclosing Party shall therefore be entitled to equitable relief in addition to all other remedies available at law.', we create an internal contradiction and limit the Disclosing Party's recourse. The 'harm' is no longer explicitly defined as irreparable, so the other remedies might not be available.
perturbation.contradicted_text: In addition, the Receiving Party acknowledges that the following specific Confidential Information, na mely: Knowledge of the identity of the Disclosing Party, coupled with the fact that that the Disclosing Part y’s practice(s) are for sale, or that the Disclosing Pa rty has commissioned appraisal(s) for the possible sale of same would, if disclosed by the Receiving Party and such information thereby came to the knowledge of STAFF (either employees or associates) of the pract ices, cause significant damages to the Disclosing P arty though departure of STAFF and/or Patients, which da mages would be difficult to quantify.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly stated that a breach would cause 'irreparable harm' and entitle the Disclosing Party to 'equitable relief in addition to all other remedies available at law.' The changed text removes 'irreparable' and removes any mention of 'equitable relief'. The Contradicted Text states that disclosure of specific information would cause 'significant damages to the Disclosing Party' which 'would be difficult to quantify.' By removing the 'irreparable harm' clause and limiting the remedies available, the changed text contradicts the contradicted text, which anticipates damages that are difficult to quantify. Without the irreparable harm clause, and reference to 'equitable relief', it is unclear what remedies are now available to the Disclosing Party to make them whole. This constitutes an Omissions - In Text Contradiction, as it removes essential language and limits the Disclosing Party's recourse and makes it difficult to enforce the damage clause.
